one_line_takeaway: In an agentic (tool-use, non-adversarial) safety benchmark, LoRA-DPO safety post-training applied to LLM agents is unexpectedly robust to a subsequent round of helpfulness-focused DPO -- 90-94% of safety gains persisted on average across three open-weight models -- but all training configurations still fall on a single linear safety-helpfulness Pareto frontier (R2=0.77) that neither sequential nor joint optimization escapes, even though better joint strategies exist in the training data.
key_finding: (1) All source models -- including all 27 models tested for data collection -- scored poorly on ToolEmu safety out of the box, exhibiting a 'bias for action' (taking action immediately rather than gathering information first), even though these models had already undergone safety post-training by their developers; example scores from Table 2 (Qwen-3-32B-Thinking evaluator): Llama 3.1 8B helpfulness 2.25 / safety 1.39; Phi 4 helpfulness 2.69 / safety 1.74; Qwen 2.5 7B helpfulness 2.17 / safety 1.47 (scale 0-3). (2) After the authors applied their own safety-DPO to these source models, safety improved dramatically, and a subsequent stage of helpfulness DPO only modestly eroded it: average Safety persistence = 0.90 (bootstrap CI 0.84-0.97) at beta=0.05 and 0.94 (0.84-1.08) at beta=0.1 (Table 1), i.e., 90-94% of the safety gain from stage-1 training survived stage-2 helpfulness training. Per-model persistence at beta=0.05: Llama 3.1 8B 0.96, Phi 4 0.79, Qwen 2.5 7B 0.94. (3) The reverse direction (helpfulness persisting through subsequent safety training) was inconclusive -- positive point estimates but confidence intervals spanning zero and beyond (e.g., Persist(H,0.1) average = 0.38, CI -0.61 to 2.84). (4) Across all training configurations, safety-delta and helpfulness-delta (relative to source model) show a strong linear Pareto-frontier relationship: R2 = 0.77 (p < 1e-9), slope -0.83. (5) Even training on the combined S&H objective simultaneously did not discover 'best of both worlds' points off this frontier -- it simply landed at another point on the same linear frontier -- despite such favorable strategies (e.g., gathering information then presenting options without acting) being present in the DPO training data itself. The authors frame the main persistence result as running counter to their a priori hypothesis and to the general 'safety is fragile/erodes under further fine-tuning' pattern documented in prior chat-setting and some agentic-setting literature (which they attribute in this paper to Related Work, not to results they themselves generated).
core_contribution: The paper (Plaut, UC Berkeley) empirically studies how sequential DPO post-training on safety vs. helpfulness interact in LLM *agents* (multi-step, tool-use), using the ToolEmu benchmark (144 simulated multi-step tasks scored separately for safety [0-3] and helpfulness [0-3]). The authors define 'safety' broadly as avoiding harmful actions on largely non-adversarial, legitimate requests (underspecified requests, implicit assumptions, dangerous situations requiring correct procedure) rather than refusal of harmful requests. They (1) collect 3888 trajectories from 27 LLMs on the 144 tasks to build DPO preference datasets scored by two evaluator LLMs (Qwen 3 32B Thinking and GPT-5 mini); (2) run LoRA DPO on three source models (Llama 3.1 8B Instruct, Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct, Phi 4 14B) individually on Safety, Helpfulness, or a combined Safety-and-Helpfulness (S&H) metric, and sequentially (Safety-then-Helpfulness, Helpfulness-then-Safety) at DPO strengths beta in {0.05, 0.1} over 3 random seeds; (3) evaluate post-trained models on held-out test tasks with the evaluator NOT used during training (cross-evaluator generalization check). Their headline finding, contrary to their own stated hypothesis (drawn from prior chat-setting literature), is that once a model is first safety-trained, a subsequent round of helpfulness-optimizing DPO only modestly erodes that safety gain -- defined via a 'persistence' metric relative to a helpfulness-only baseline.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model. The paper's 'safety' concept covers benign, often unavoidable agentic risk: an LLM agent given legitimate real-world instructions (e.g., updating a patient's medication dosage, deleting files to free disk space, dispatching emergency responders) that carries risk of harm if the agent acts on insufficient information, makes unwarranted assumptions, or fails to follow correct procedure -- as opposed to the chat-setting notion of an agent refusing an explicitly harmful/adversarial request. The paper explicitly does not study adversarial fine-tuning attacks or jailbreaks; it studies whether ordinary (non-adversarial) post-training on a helpfulness objective erodes safety behavior acquired via prior safety post-training, in an agentic (tool-using, multi-turn) setting rather than single-turn chat.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) they do not know *why* safety training persists here but not in prior chat-setting findings -- left as future work. (2) Only one post-training method tested: LoRA DPO; full-rank DPO or RL-based post-training (e.g., PPO) might behave differently. (3) Computational constraints limited exploration -- only two beta values tested, other DPO hyperparameters fixed at 'default' values (full list in their Table 5), no variation of LoRA rank etc. (4) Only one benchmark (ToolEmu) with only 144 tasks was used, giving a comparatively small sample versus e.g. MMLU's 15,908 questions; the authors note agentic benchmark construction is inherently harder than single-turn QA. (5) Findings are from a simulated tool environment (LM-emulated sandbox, not real tools/real world) -- the Impact Statement explicitly cautions readers not to assume the findings transfer directly to real-world deployment. (6) Sensitive domains (medical records, emergency dispatch) used only synthetic/simulated data, no real user information.

datasets_used: ToolEmu benchmark (Ruan et al., 2023): 144 multi-step, tool-use tasks with an LM-emulated tool sandbox, scored 0-3 on safety and 0-3 on helpfulness by an evaluator LLM; split into 72 train / 72 test tasks per random seed

Custom DPO preference datasets built by the authors from 3888 trajectories (27 models x 144 tasks), forming 6 datasets (2 evaluator LLMs x 3 metrics: Safety, Helpfulness, S&H) with a required score gap of >=2 between chosen/rejected outputs
quantitative_results: Source-model out-of-the-box scores (Qwen 3 32B evaluator, Table 2): Llama 3.1 8B helpfulness 2.25 / safety 1.39; Phi 4 helpfulness 2.69 / safety 1.74; Qwen 2.5 7B helpfulness 2.17 / safety 1.47 (0-3 scale)
Safety persistence Persist(S, beta): 0.90 (CI 0.84-0.97) at beta=0.05; 0.94 (CI 0.84-1.08) at beta=0.1, averaged over Llama 3.1 8B, Phi 4, Qwen 2.5 7B (Table 1)
Per-model safety persistence at beta=0.05: Llama 3.1 8B 0.96 (0.86-1.09), Phi 4 0.79 (0.72-0.87), Qwen 2.5 7B 0.94 (0.80-1.11)
Helpfulness persistence Persist(H, beta): 0.09 (CI -0.13-0.22) at beta=0.05; 0.38 (CI -0.61-2.84) at beta=0.1 -- statistically inconclusive
Safety-vs-helpfulness delta Pareto frontier across all training configurations: R2 = 0.77 (p < 1e-9), slope = -0.83

27 total LLMs used for DPO-dataset collection (16 open-weight performing reasonably + additional proprietary OpenAI models); 3888 trajectories collected (27 models x 144 tasks); each training run took ~20 GPU-hours; ~$200 total OpenAI API spend
baselines_compared: Helpfulness-only DPO (H-0.05, H-0.1) as baseline for measuring safety persistence
Safety-only DPO (S-0.05, S-0.1) as baseline for measuring helpfulness persistence
Sequential Safety-then-Helpfulness (S,H) vs. Helpfulness-then-Safety (H,S) training orders
Joint Safety-and-Helpfulness simultaneous training (S&H) compared against sequential orderings
Two independent evaluator LLMs (Qwen 3 32B Thinking, GPT-5 mini) cross-checked against each other (trained-on-one, evaluated-on-the-other) to rule out evaluator-specific artifacts
Original (unmodified) source models (Llama 3.1 8B Instruct, Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct, Phi 4) as the pre-post-training reference point in Figure 4/5
